perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, compensation for each calendar quarter will not exceed [ ].
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, compensation for each calendar quarter will not exceed [ ]. The payment of compensation to Company is entirely discretionary and may be reduced or eliminated at any time without notice.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a clause that makes compensation entirely discretionary, the agreement creates uncertainty regarding payment for services already rendered. This directly contradicts Massachusetts law regarding fair business practices and payment for services rendered.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 149, § 148 requires timely and consistent payment of wages for services rendered. The modified text introduces a clause making compensation discretionary, allowing FIIOC to potentially avoid paying for services already completed by NYLIAC, which is a violation of the Massachusetts Wage Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 148 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 148 Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 148: Payment of wages; commissions; exemption by contract; persons deemed employers; provision for cashing check or draft; violation of statute Section 148. Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him to within six days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if employed for five or six days in a calendar week, or to within seven days of the termination of the pay period during which the wages were earned if such employee is employed seven days in a calendar week, or in the case of an employee who has worked for a period of less than five days, hereinafter called a casual employee, shall, within seven days after the termination of such period, pay the wages earned by such casual employee during such period, but any employee leaving his employment shall be paid in full on the following regular pay day, and, in the absence of a regular pay day, on the following Saturday; and any employee discharged from such employment shall be paid in full on the day of his discharge, or in Boston as soon as the laws requiring pay rolls, bills and accounts to be certified shall have been complied with; and the commonwealth, its departments, officers, boards and commissions shall so pay every mechanic, workman and laborer employed by it or them, and every person employed in any other capacity by it or them in any penal or charitable institution, and every county and city shall so pay every employee engaged in its business the wag
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause making compensation discretionary, which directly contradicts M.G.L. c. 149, § 148, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original contract guarantees compensation for each calendar quarter, while the changed text allows the company to avoid paying for services already completed. The scraped law snippet states, "Every person having employees in his service shall pay weekly or bi-weekly each such employee the wages earned by him", which implies that compensation must be paid for services rendered. The addition of discretionary payment undermines this legal requirement, creating a direct conflict with the law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Applicable Law. This Agreement shall be construed and the provisions hereof interpreted under and in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Applicable Law. This Agreement shall be construed and the provisions hereof interpreted under and in accordance with the laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the applicable law from Massachusetts to Delaware potentially impacts consumer protections afforded to Massachusetts residents under Massachusetts law. This is particularly relevant if Delaware law provides weaker consumer protections in specific areas relevant to the services provided under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A provides broad consumer protection in Massachusetts. Changing the governing law to Delaware could weaken these protections for Massachusetts residents, as Delaware's consumer protection laws may not be as comprehensive. The original agreement applies to services provided to Massachusetts residents (beneficial interest in annuity or life insurance products), so the change exposes them to potential reduction in rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 1 Definitions Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Definitions Section 1. The following words, as used in this chapter unless the text otherwise requires or a different meaning is specifically required, shall mean— (a) ''Person'' shall include, where applicable, natural persons, corporations, trusts, partnerships, incorporated or unincorporated associations, and any other legal entity. (b) ''Trade'' and ''commerce'' shall include the advertising, the offering for sale, rent or lease, the sale, rent, lease or distribution of any services and any property, tangible or intangible, real, personal or mixed, any security as defined in subparagraph (k) of section four hundred and one of chapter one hundred and ten A and any contract of sale of a commodity for future delivery, and any other article, commodity, or thing of value wherever situate, and shall include any trade or commerce directly or indirectly affecting the people of this commonwealth. (c) ''Documentary material'' shall include the original or a copy of any book, record, report, memorandum, paper, communication, tabulation, map, chart, photograph, mechanical transcription, or other tangible document or recording, wherever situate. (d) ''Examination of documentary material'', the inspection, study, or copying of any such material, and the taking of testimony under oath or acknowledgment in respect of any such documentary material. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modification changes the applicable law from Massachusetts to Delaware, which contradicts the consumer protections afforded under Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, as described in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade' and 'commerce' as including the sale of services and property affecting the people of the commonwealth, indicating Massachusetts' intent to protect its residents. By changing the applicable law, the altered contract weakens the consumer protections for Massachusetts residents, constituting an omission of a legally mandated consumer protection, which is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned without the written consent of the other party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, except that it shall be assigned automatically to any successor to FIIOC as the Funds' transfer agent, and any such successor shall be bound by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be assigned without the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld' and the automatic assignment to successors may allow for assignment to entities that are not qualified or compliant, potentially harming the annuity contract holders. By removing that the successor must comply with the terms of the agreement, it introduces a risk that consumer rights will be violated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Commercial Code - Assignment of Rights (M.G.L. c. 106, § 2-210)
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 106, § 2-210 addresses assignment of rights in contracts and implies a duty of good faith. Allowing assignment without any constraint (reasonableness or successor compliance) could be interpreted as violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law, especially if the assignment negatively impacts the consumer's rights and protections under the annuity contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-210 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-210 Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights Section 2-210 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-210: Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights Section 2–210. (1) A party may perform his duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract. No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Except as otherwise provided in Section 9–406, unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (21/2) The creation, attachment, perfection, or enforcement of a security interest in the seller's interest under a contract is not a transfer that materially changes the duty of or increases materially the burden or risk imposed on the buyer or impairs materially the buyer's chance of obtaining return performance within the purview of subsection (2) unless, and then only to the extent that, enforcement actually results in a delegation of material performance of the seller. Even in that event, the creation, attachment, perfection, and enforcement of the security interest remain effective, but (i) the seller is liable to the buyer for damages caused by th
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated that assignment could not be unreasonably withheld and that successors would be bound by the terms of the agreement. The changed text eliminates both conditions, allowing assignment without any constraint. The Law Explanation suggests this violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if it harms consumer rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, M.G.L. c. 106, § 2-210, states that rights can be assigned unless the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. By removing the 'reasonableness' and 'successor compliance' clauses, the changed text increases the risk and burden on the annuity contract holders, contradicting the principle outlined in the law snippet. The omission of these protections makes the contract potentially non-compliant as it removes legally relevant consumer protection.
